docs.title: Welcome
docs.text:
Lia Ryan's Datasheet as part of Autocan for Team 202

Submission: 10 - 27 - 2025

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: • This datasheet site shows the progress and steps taken to produce the External IR sensor and UI lights subsystem schematic for Team 202's Autocan: Automatic Trash Can project. This subsystem detects the user's desire to open the trash can and communicates what state the device is in through LEDs.

docs.title: Project Summary
docs.text:
• For our (Team 202's) project, we decided to design an automatic trash can that improved on the shortcomings of the trash cans that are currently on the market. We wanted to make our device as helpful as possible to the busy homeowner who may have pets or children while keeping any unpleasant smell contained. To accomplish this, we used an external IR sensor to detect when the user wants to thrw trash into the device. Then the IR sensor will send a signal to the motorized lid to open as quickly as possible whilst also blinking a blue LED to inform the user that an action is taking place. For extra convenience, a weight sensor and internal IR sensor will be used to detect when the trash is almost too heavy for the bag or reaching the top of the can respectively. These sensors will send a signal to the External IR sensor and LEDs to turn on a red LED, notifying the user to take out the trash.
  • The full team report can be found here.

docs.title: My Contribution
docs.text:
My part in the Autocan project was to design the external IR sensor and UI lights. To do this I did research and designed parts that would function well together to complete this taks. The following are the major steps I have taken in designing my subsystem.

  • To view my Block Diagram, click here. This part of the design process shows the general idea of how my PCB will function and the connections to each sensor it will use to carry out the required task.
  • To view my Component Selection, click here. This part of the design process displays the major pieces that I need to obtain in order to create my subsystem. Also, showing the array of parts I reviewed to find the part that would fit my design best
  • To view my Schematic, click here. This part of the design process shows my schematic for my subsystem that was created using KiCad 9.0.
  • To view my Power Budget, click here. This part of the design process shows the power that all of my parts are expected to draw in order to choose the correct value fuse for the power supply.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This block diagram uses an IR LED, OP-amp, and Phototransistor to create an IR sensor. This IR sensor will detect when a user wishes to open the trash can. When the Curiosity Nano detects this input from the sensor, it will then send an opening digital signal to the motor subsystem through the communication connector to open the lid.

Along with the exterior IR sensor, this subsystem also controls an RGB light that will inform the user of amount of trash in the trash can. If the Curiosity Nano receives a full signal from the weight sensor or the interior IR sensor through the communication connector, the front LED will start blinking red, informing the user to replace or compact the trash.

The power source for this subsystem will be a 9V power supply from a wall connected barrel-jack, or a 9V and 5V transfer from another subsystem through the communication connectors.

docs.title: Meeting Criteria
docs.text: Using a powerful IR LED and phototransistor will allow the user to interact with the trash can from a distance of 2 feet. The quick response of the IR sensor will allow the motor subsystem 2 seconds to open the lid for the user. The 3 LEDs will inform the user of state of the trash can, whether full, in the process of moving, or malfunctioning.

docs.title: External IR Sensor and User Interface Lights Block Diagram
docs.text: Here is the block diagram for Lia Ryan's Subsystem.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The following are the components I have selected for my subsystem and the reason as to why I chose them.

docs.title: Photo Transistor
docs.text:
Solution Pros Cons Option 1 Vishay BPW34 Through Hole Photo Transistor $1.23 each link * Small Size * More precise distance * More expensive * Higher viewing angle Solution Pros Cons Option 2 Vishay BPW96B Through Hole Photo Transistor *$0.95 each link Less expensive Lower Viewing angle Less precise distance Large size Solution Pros Cons Option 3 TT Electronics OPB732 Through Hole IR LED $4.61 each link • Currently have 1 device• Setup is known • Short viewing distance• More expensive

Choice: Option 2: Vishay BPW96B Through Hole Photo Transistor

Rationale: For our products operation we don’t need a precise distance calculation. As long as the IR sensor can detect an object within a set range, the product’s operation can be activated. Also, option 2 will be able to pick up the signals we want without interference due to its lower viewing angle. Option 2 is less expensive than the other choices, allowing our customers to pay less for the finished device.

docs.title: IR LED
docs.text:
Solution Pros Cons Option 1TSAL6100 Through Hole IR LED$0.49 eachlink • High radiant intensity• Lower viewing angle • Need to construct housing• Need to create an attachment to PIC Solution Pros Cons Option 2 TSAL6200 Through Hole IR LED$0.49 each link • Higher stock• Stable over operating temperature • Higher viewing angle• Lower radiant intensity• Need to construct housing• Need to create an attachment to PIC Solution Pros Cons Option 3TT Electronics OPB732 Through Hole IR LED$4.61 eachlink • Currently have 1 device• Setup is known • Short viewing distance• Expensive

Choice: Option 1: TSAL6100 Through Hole IR LED

Rationale: This LED Is easier to work with and should give more exact results than the other options. The product shouldn’t be triggered automatically; with the narrow viewing angle of this LED, only a small area will be the trigger area compared to the TSAL6200. Also, the range of this LED is far greater than the OPB732, making operation of the product easier for the end user.

docs.title: Op Amp for Photo Transistor
docs.text:
Solution Pros Cons Option 1Microchip MCP6022-I/P Through Hole Op Amp$1.86 eachlink • High frequency gain bandwidth• Has the number of circuits needed • Higher price• Slow shipping speed Solution Pros Cons Option 2Analog Devices OP295GPZ Through Hole Op Amp$12.82 eachlink • Has the number of circuits needed• Smaller size • Higher price• Lower frequency gain bandwidth Solution Pros Cons Option 3Microchip MCP6004-I/P Through Hole Op Amp$0.59 eachlink • Lower price• Currently have 1 device • Lower frequency gain bandwidth• Has more circuits than necessary

Choice: Option 3 Microchip MCP6004-I/P Through hole Op Amp

Rationale: The MCP6004-I/P is easier to work with because we have worked with in the past and know the general setup that is required to make it function. Making our product more affordable for the customer, the op-amp is significantly less expensive than the competitors and will be able to complete its task.

docs.title: Red LED
docs.text:
Solution Pros Cons Option 1Lumimax LEDDC-5RED Through Hole LED$0.08 eachlink • Less expensive• Less pins• Only one color • Clear Solution Pros Cons Option 2QTB QBL8RGB60D0-2897 Through Hole LED$1.06 eachlink • Multiple colors• Diffused • More expensive• More complicated to code Solution Pros Cons Option 3QTB QBL8RGB25C0 Through Hole LED$1.16 eachlink • Multiple colors• Only takes a single LED housing instead of 2 • More expensive• Clear

Choice: Option 2: QTB QBL8RGB60D0-2897 Through Hole LED

Rationale: An RGB LED will only take up on spot on the product making it look nicer than if it were two separate LEDS of differing colors. The diffused housing of option 2 hides the electronics inside the LED housing making the product more visually appealing.

docs.title: Blue LED
docs.text: Using RGB LED

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This schematic is primarily designed to support the external IR sensor of the AutoCan. This sensor will detect when a user wishes to open the trash can and send a signal to the motor subsystem to open. This schematic also has front facing LEDs to inform the user if the level or weight of the trash inside reachces a critical amount and requires taking out.

Figure 01: Showing Lia Ryan's Exterior IR sensor Schematic.

docs.text: The schematic as a PDF download is available here, and the Zip folder of the project here.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This is the front and back of my PCB design without the drill holes. Both show the copper that will remain after milling. However, the front also shows the Top silkscreen. Using jumpers, this PCB is modular and somewhat reconfigurable in the case that mistakes were make during design.

Only the power supply was able to be soldered due to time constraints from receiving the PCB late and delays due to testing the power supply.

Figure 02: Showing Lia Ryan's Back PCB Design.

Figure 03: Showing Lia Ryan's Front PCB without any parts soldered.

Figure 04: Showing Lia Ryan's Back PCB without any parts soldered.

Figure 03: Showing Lia Ryan's Front PCB with the power supply soldered.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This is the code used in my IR sensor subsystem to interact with the other subsystems and the user. This code allows my subsytem to inform the speaker and motor subsystems to activate when the user waves their hand in front of the IR sensor. Also, when the weight subsystem detects there is too much trash in the trashcan, my subsystem will inform the user through a red LED.

docs.title: Download
docs.text: The MPLabX code used for my subsystem can be downloaded here.

docs.title: Ideas for Hardware Improvement
docs.text:
If I were do redo this project again, there would be several things in which I would improve upon. The first, would be the spacing and labeling of the jumpers and components on my PCB. As shown in PCB pictures in the PCB Design page, I was not able to finish soldering my components to my PCB board. Part of this is due to how much longer my team's PCB shipment took to ship compared to other. Also, the positioning of my parts on the board were pretty cramped. This made it difficult to solder pieces onto the board that were close together. To fix this issue in the future, I would plan out how I would solder my parts to the board and position them further apart. I was only able to solder my power supply onto my board due to me believing that I messed it up as the connection that was supposed to by supplying 5V was instead supplying 9V. This happened because I closed a jumper that would connect my 5V and 9V together if my teammates and I were sharing power amongst each other. To fix this, I would label important jumpers using the silkscreen so I would be more knowledgable on which ones might cause my system to not function.

Another change that I would make to improve the hardware would be 3D printing a housing for my IR LED and phototransistor. Currently, the components for the IR sensor would be attached to the PCB through screw terminals and wires. This wouldn't work very well for a product as the transmitter and receiver of the sensor wouldn't be in alignment. Creating a housing for both parts would solve this issue and make it easier to mount on a trash can.
